charts.title: Topic Map
charts.description: Overview of t-norms, t-conorms, the idempotent set, and the Mostert-Shields classification of all continuous t-norms and t-conorms.

nodes.label:
Week 7
Mostert-Shields
nodes.note: Week 7 classifies all continuous t-norms and t-conorms. The Mostert-Shields theorems show that any continuous conjunction or disjunction is a chimera of the three standard fuzzy operators.

nodes.label: T-norms
nodes.note: A t-norm is a binary operation on [0,1] that generalises Boolean conjunction. It must satisfy the four properties C1-C4: boolean, increasing, continuous, and associative.

nodes.label: T-conorms
nodes.note: A t-conorm is the dual binary operation generalising disjunction. It must satisfy D1-D4 and is related to a t-norm by neg(neg(x) and neg(y)).

nodes.label:
Three Standard
T-norms
nodes.note: F1 = min, F2 = xy, and F3 = max(0,x+y-1) are the three canonical t-norms. They are not isomorphic to each other and serve as building blocks.

nodes.label:
Idempotent
Set A
nodes.note: The set A = {z : z and z = z} of fixed points of the t-norm. Its structure determines how the t-norm decomposes into sub-intervals.

nodes.label:
Mostert-Shields
Theorem
nodes.note: Every continuous t-norm acts as min outside its idempotent intervals and is isomorphic to either F2 or F3 on each maximal non-idempotent sub-interval.

nodes.label: Chimera
nodes.note: A chimera t-norm is one that locally looks like F1, F2 or F3 in different regions of [0,1]^2. Any continuous t-norm is a chimera of these three.

nodes.label:
Lemmas
6.2-6.4
nodes.note: The proof of Mostert-Shields proceeds via Lemma 6.2 (min behaviour near idempotents), Lemma 6.3 (type 2 sub-intervals isomorphic to product), and Lemma 6.4 (type 3 isomorphic to Lukasiewicz).

nodes.label: C1-C4 Axioms
nodes.note: C1 boolean boundary, C2 monotone increasing in each component, C3 continuous, C4 associative. Together they ensure the t-norm behaves like a reasonable conjunction.

nodes.label:
Type 2
Sub-interval
nodes.note: Type 2 intervals [a,b] are those where z and z is never equal to a for z in the open interval. They are isomorphic to the product t-norm F2.

nodes.label:
Type 3
Sub-interval
nodes.note: Type 3 intervals [a,b] are those where some z in the open interval has z and z = a. They are isomorphic to the Lukasiewicz t-norm F3.

nodes.label:
Limit-
commutativity
nodes.note: Continuity (C3) implies that the t-norm commutes with limits of convergent sequences: lim x_n and y = lim (x_n and y).

nodes.label:
Conjunction-
Disjunction Duality
nodes.note: Any t-conorm is obtained from a t-norm by the De Morgan-like formula x or y = 1 - ((1-x) and (1-y)). This makes Theorem 6.6 follow from Theorem 6.1.

charts.title: Verifying a T-norm (C1-C4)
charts.description: Decision procedure to check whether a candidate binary function on [0,1] is a continuous t-norm.

nodes.label:
Candidate
operation and
nodes.note: We are given a binary function and : [0,1]^2 -> [0,1] and want to verify whether it satisfies all four t-norm axioms.

nodes.label:
C1: correct on
0 and 1?
nodes.note: Plug in the four corners (0,0), (0,1), (1,0), (1,1) and check that the values match classical conjunction.

nodes.label: Not a t-norm
nodes.note: At least one axiom has failed, so the candidate is not a continuous t-norm. Stop and report the failing axiom.

nodes.label:
C2: increasing in
each component?
nodes.note: Show that fixing one argument and increasing the other never decreases the result. This is monotonicity in both slots.

nodes.label:
C3: continuous on
[0,1]^2 ?
nodes.note: Verify continuity using epsilon-delta or by composing continuous building blocks. Pay attention to boundaries between piecewise regions.

nodes.label: C4: associative?
nodes.note: Compute (x and y) and z and x and (y and z) symbolically and check they are equal. Often easiest by case analysis.

nodes.label:
Continuous t-norm
verified
nodes.note: All four axioms hold, so the function is a continuous t-norm. By Mostert-Shields it is a chimera of F1, F2, and F3.

charts.title: Mostert-Shields Classification of a Sub-interval
charts.description: Procedure for deciding which canonical t-norm a maximal non-idempotent sub-interval is isomorphic to.

nodes.label: T-norm and on [0,1]
nodes.note: We have a continuous t-norm and want to apply Mostert-Shields to classify its behaviour on every sub-interval of [0,1].

nodes.label:
Compute idempotent
set A
nodes.note: A = { z : z and z = z }. Always contains 0 and 1. Its structure determines where the t-norm acts as min.

nodes.label:
On the complement
of sub-intervals
and = min
nodes.note: Outside the maximal sub-intervals [a_i,b_i] with endpoints in A and no idempotents in between, the t-norm equals min by Lemma 6.2.

nodes.label:
Find a maximal
sub-interval [a,b]
nodes.note: Pick a maximal interval whose endpoints are in A but whose interior contains no idempotents. Each such interval needs separate classification.

nodes.label:
Exists z in (a,b)
with z and z = a?
nodes.note: Check whether the lower endpoint a is reached by squaring some interior element. This single test distinguishes type 2 from type 3 intervals.

nodes.label:
Type 2
isomorphic to F2 (xy)
nodes.note: Lemma 6.3: the sub-interval is isomorphic to the product t-norm. The isomorphism is built using and-exponentiation and logarithms.

nodes.label:
Type 3
isomorphic to F3 (Lukasiewicz)
nodes.note: Lemma 6.4: the sub-interval is isomorphic to the Lukasiewicz t-norm. The construction uses an order-reversing isomorphism.

nodes.label:
More sub-intervals
remain?
nodes.note: Continue until every maximal non-idempotent sub-interval has been classified. Each one independently chooses type 2 or type 3.

nodes.label:
Chimera fully
described
nodes.note: Every region of [0,1] has been classified: outside the sub-intervals the t-norm acts as min, and within each sub-interval it acts like F2 or F3.

charts.title: Three Canonical T-norms - Comparison
charts.description: Taxonomy of the three standard continuous t-norms, their idempotent sets, and the conjugate t-conorms.

nodes.label:
Standard
T-norms
nodes.note: Three canonical t-norms generate every continuous t-norm via the chimera construction. They are mutually non-isomorphic.

nodes.label: F1 = min(x,y)
nodes.note: Godel t-norm. Every element is idempotent: z and z = z for all z. This is the only t-norm with idempotent set equal to all of [0,1].

nodes.label: F2 = x * y
nodes.note: Product t-norm. Only 0 and 1 are idempotent, and z and z is strictly less than z for any z in (0,1).

nodes.label: F3 = max(0,x+y-1)
nodes.note: Lukasiewicz t-norm. Has 0 and 1 as idempotents but additionally 0.5 and 0.5 = 0, so it produces type 3 sub-intervals.

nodes.label: Conorm: max(x,y)
nodes.note: The dual to F1: maximum is the simplest disjunction operator and arises by De Morgan from min.

nodes.label: Conorm: x+y-x*y
nodes.note: The dual to F2: probability sum, also called inclusion-exclusion. It is the De Morgan dual of the product t-norm.

nodes.label: Conorm: min(1,x+y)
nodes.note: The dual to F3: bounded sum. This is the Lukasiewicz disjunction, the only one validating excluded middle.

nodes.label: A = [0,1]
nodes.note: All elements are idempotent for F1, so the chimera classification immediately gives the trivial decomposition with no sub-intervals.

nodes.label: A = {0,1}
nodes.note: For F2 and F3 only the endpoints are idempotent, so the entire interval [0,1] is one maximal sub-interval.

charts.title: Lemma 6.2 - Min Behaviour Near Idempotents
charts.description: Proof outline showing that the t-norm acts as min when one of its arguments lies between an idempotent.

nodes.label:
z in A and
x <= z <= y
nodes.note: We are given a t-norm idempotent z, and want to show that x and y reduces to min(x,y) whenever x <= z <= y.

nodes.label:
Use IVT to find
t with x = z and t
nodes.note: Continuity (C3) and monotonicity together with the idempotency of z give the existence of some t in [0,1] with z and t = x.

nodes.label:
Rewrite x =
(z and z) and t
nodes.note: Substitute z and z = z (since z is idempotent) into the equation x = z and t to obtain x = (z and z) and t.

nodes.label:
Apply C4
associativity
nodes.note: By associativity (C4), (z and z) and t = z and (z and t) = z and x. So x equals z and x.

nodes.label:
C2: z <= y so
z and x <= y and x
nodes.note: Apply monotonicity in the first argument: replacing z by the larger value y gives z and x <= y and x = x and y.

nodes.label:
C2 again:
y and x <= 1 and x
nodes.note: Apply monotonicity again: y and x <= 1 and x. By the boundary axioms 1 and x = x, so y and x <= x.

nodes.label: x and y = x = min(x,y)
nodes.note: Combining the two inequalities x <= y and x <= x gives equality. So the t-norm equals min on this region, which is exactly Lemma 6.2.
